description: A maximum of 16 tunnels can be up simultaneously.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.15.1050, for HPE Aruba AOS-CX release 10.15.1050, what is the caveat for the 6-in-6 feature?
Feature Caveat: 6-in-6 - A maximum of 16 tunnels can be up simultaneously.

description: If there is no user configuration for DNS servers and HPE Aruba Networking Central is configured, the switch defaults to using Google's DNS server at 8.8.8.8. HPE Aruba Networking Central is enabled by default across all switch platforms

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.15.1050, what caveat is documented for Activate?

Feature Caveat: Activate - If there is no user configuration for DNS servers and HPE Aruba Networking Central is configured, the switch defaults to using Google's DNS server at 8.8.8.8. HPE Aruba Networking Central is enabled by default across all switch platforms.

description: The next-hop-unchanged option needs to be explicitly configured to preserve nexthop while advertising routes to eBGP peers, in the L2VPN EVPN address-family. For example: Copy router bgp

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.15.1050, what caveat is documented for BGP?

Feature Caveat: BGP - The next-hop-unchanged option needs to be explicitly configured to preserve the nexthop while advertising routes to eBGP peers in the L2VPN EVPN address-family. For example: Copy router bgp.

description: Symptom: Excessive connection lost and connection established logging when multiple NTP servers are configured. Scenario: If the NTP server changes, a connection lost and connection established is logged to the event log. Workaround: The fix disables logging of normal server changes and only logs events if server reachability reaches 0 or NTP synchronization is lost.
symptom: Excessive connection lost and connection established logging when multiple NTP servers are configured.
scenario: If the NTP server changes, a connection lost and connection established is logged to the event log.
workaround: The fix disables logging of normal server changes and only logs events if server reachability reaches 0 or NTP synchronization is lost.

description: Symptom: There is a resource leak when an L3 host (ARP / ND) moves between local port (non-VxLAN) to VxLAN tunnel. Over a period of time and especially under scaled L3 hosts environments, this slow resource leak may lead the system to run out of L3 resources critical for neighbor and route forwarding, leading to L3 connectivity issues. Scenario: With VxLAN deployments, if a host moves between a local port to VxLAN tunnel, there is a critical resource leak with every host move. When there are a large number of mobile wireless devices changing wireless access point connections, specifically between locally connected (non-VxLAN) and over VxLAN tunnels, L3 resources needed for neighbor / route forwarding may run out. Workaround: Reboot the box to reclaim leaked resources. In a VSX deploymeny, reboot the secondary device, then reboot the primary device after the secondary device comes back up to keep downtime minimal.
symptom: There is a resource leak when an L3 host (ARP / ND) moves between local port (non-VxLAN) to VxLAN tunnel. Over a period of time and especially under scaled L3 hosts environments, this slow resource leak may lead the system to run out of L3 resources critical for neighbor and route forwarding, leading to L3 connectivity issues.

workaround: Reboot the box to reclaim leaked resources. In a VSX deploymeny, reboot the secondary device, then reboot the primary device after the secondary device comes back up to keep downtime minimal.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.15.1050, for HPE Aruba AOS-CX 10.15.1050, what issue was resolved in the EVPN feature related to Bug ID 379727?

EVPN (Bug ID 379727): Symptom: There is a resource leak when an L3 host (ARP / ND) moves between local port (non-VxLAN) and VxLAN tunnel. Over time, especially in scaled environments with many mobile wireless devices changing access points, this slow resource leak can exhaust critical L3 resources needed for neighbor and route forwarding, leading to connectivity issues. Scenario: In VxLAN deployments, if a host moves between a local port to a VxLAN tunnel, there is a critical resource leak with each move. This issue becomes more pronounced in environments with many mobile devices switching access points. Workaround: Reboot the device to reclaim leaked resources. In a VSX deployment, reboot the secondary device first, then the primary after the secondary comes back up to minimize downtime.
